author: Dr. Michael Torres
chiefComplaint: Nephrology follow-up for CKD Stage 3b management
hpi:
69-year-old male with CKD Stage 3b (likely diabetic nephropathy and hypertensive nephrosclerosis), HFrEF (EF 30-35%), persistent A-fib on warfarin, and Type 2 DM presents for routine nephrology evaluation. Last nephrology visit was approximately 6 months ago. Since then, his renal function has remained relatively stable. Most recent labs (9/24/2021) showed Cr 1.88, eGFR 38, K 4.7, Na 137. UACR from July was 150 mg/g (A2 stage, moderately increased albuminuria).

Patient has had a moderately eventful past year including a pneumonia hospitalization in February 2021, a UTI in September treated with nitrofurantoin, and recurrent mild heart failure volume overload episodes managed with temporary furosemide increases. His heart failure team has been uptitrating carvedilol — he is now on 12.5mg AM / 25mg PM and plans to reach target dose of 25mg BID. He remains on Entresto 97/103mg BID and spironolactone 25mg daily.

From a renal standpoint, he has tolerated the neurohormonal blockade (Entresto + spironolactone) without hyperkalemia or significant Cr elevation. His eGFR has been slowly declining at approximately 2-3 mL/min/year over the past 3 years (eGFR 42 in early 2018, now 38). He denies any new edema-related symptoms beyond his usual heart failure fluctuations. He follows a low-sodium diet. His wife manages his medications and monitors daily weights. He was counseled on NSAID avoidance previously and confirms he has avoided them.
pastMedicalHistory:
1. Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3b, eGFR 38 (diagnosed 2018)
2. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus with peripheral neuropathy (diagnosed 2010)
3. Heart Failure with Reduced EF, 30-35% (diagnosed 2019)
4. Atrial Fibrillation, persistent, on warfarin (diagnosed 2017)
5. Hypertension (diagnosed 2005)
6. Hyperlipidemia (diagnosed 2008)
7. Coronary Artery Disease, 3-vessel (2018)
8. BPH (diagnosed 2016)
9. Vitamin D deficiency (2019)
10. History of gout (2019)
11. History of CAP (February 2021)
12. ACE inhibitor angioedema (lisinopril, 2018)
medications:
1. Sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto) 97/103mg BID
2. Carvedilol 12.5mg AM / 25mg PM (being uptitrated to 25mg BID)
3. Spironolactone 25mg daily
4. Furosemide 40mg BID
5. Warfarin 5mg daily
6. Insulin glargine 22 units QHS (recently increased from 20)
7. Metformin 500mg BID (renal-dosed)
8. Atorvastatin 80mg daily
9. Gabapentin 300mg TID
10. Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily
11. Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily
socialHistory: Former smoker, quit 1995 (20 pack-year). Rare alcohol. Retired accountant. Lives with wife Patricia who manages medications and daily weights. Two adult children nearby.
familyHistory: Father: HTN, MI age 62, deceased 74. Mother: T2DM, deceased 82. Brother: HTN. Sister: T2DM. No family history of polycystic kidney disease or other hereditary renal disease.

reviewOfSystems.Constitutional: Chronic fatigue (multifactorial: HF, CKD-related anemia, deconditioning). No fevers. Weight 98.2 kg today — near baseline.
reviewOfSystems.Cardiovascular: Baseline DOE (NYHA II), stable. No orthopnea currently. Some palpitations (known A-fib). Leg swelling at baseline trace level.
reviewOfSystems.GI: Mild intermittent epigastric discomfort — being evaluated by GI (Dr. Thompson). Appetite fair. No nausea or vomiting.
reviewOfSystems.GU: Nocturia x2. Urine output adequate per patient. No hematuria. No foamy urine noted by patient. UTI in September resolved.
reviewOfSystems.Musculoskeletal: No bone pain. No muscle cramps — sometimes a concern with electrolyte abnormalities in CKD.
reviewOfSystems.Neurologic: Bilateral foot numbness (diabetic neuropathy), stable. No new weakness or cognitive changes.
reviewOfSystems.Skin: No pruritus (can be a uremic symptom). No easy bruising. No skin changes.

physicalExam.General: Alert, oriented, pleasant obese male. Slightly fatigued appearance (chronic). No acute distress. BMI ~31.6.
physicalExam.HEENT: Normocephalic. Sclera anicteric. Conjunctivae mildly pale (consistent with anemia). Oropharynx clear. No uremic fetor. JVP 7 cm H2O — not elevated.
physicalExam.Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular (A-fib). Rate ~84 bpm. S1/S2 present. Systolic murmur at apex (known MR). No pericardial rub.
physicalExam.Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezes.
physicalExam.Abdomen: Soft, obese, non-tender. No renal bruits bilaterally (auscultated specifically). No hepatomegaly. No CVA tenderness.
physicalExam.Extremities: Trace bilateral ankle edema — at his baseline. No asterixis (assessed). Skin intact, no excoriations. Toenails thickened bilaterally (onychomycosis).
physicalExam.Neurologic: Alert, oriented x4. Decreased sensation bilateral feet (known neuropathy). No asterixis. No myoclonus.

assessment.diagnosis: Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3b, likely diabetic nephropathy and hypertensive nephrosclerosis
assessment.notes: eGFR 38 (Cr 1.88), stable over past 6 months. Rate of decline approximately 2-3 mL/min/year over past 3 years. UACR 150 mg/g (A2 — moderately increased albuminuria, stable). Currently on ARNI (Entresto) which provides some renal protection. No evidence of acute kidney injury or accelerated decline. CKD complications developing but manageable: mild anemia (Hgb 11.0), potassium trending at upper normal range (4.7-4.8), bicarbonate at low normal (22). Vitamin D being supplemented. No overt secondary hyperparathyroidism symptoms — PTH not recently checked.

K 4.7 — acceptable but at the higher end. Patient on Entresto (ARNI) and spironolactone (MRA), both of which raise potassium. With carvedilol being uptitrated, must monitor closely. Threshold for concern: K >5.5. Current level safe but trending warrants continued surveillance.

Hgb 11.0 — stable mild anemia. Iron studies not recently checked. Per KDIGO guidelines, ESA therapy generally considered when Hgb consistently <10 after iron stores optimized. Not yet at that threshold. Iron studies should be obtained.
A1c 7.8%, trending up. Glycemic control directly impacts CKD progression. SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin) would provide dual benefit for CKD and HFrEF — will discuss with cardiology.

plan.action: Recheck CMP, phosphorus, intact PTH, and UACR today. eGFR stable at current trajectory — at this rate, will reach CKD Stage 4 (eGFR <30) in approximately 3-4 years. No imminent dialysis concerns (dialysis planning typically begins eGFR <20). Continue all current renal-protective medications. Continue avoiding nephrotoxins — counsel patient again to avoid NSAIDs, limit contrast exposure.

Order iron studies (ferritin, TSAT). If iron deficient (ferritin <100 or TSAT <20%), will start IV iron supplementation. If iron replete and Hgb drops below 10, will consider erythropoietin-stimulating agent. Target Hgb 10-11.5 g/dL per KDIGO.

K 4.7 currently acceptable. With carvedilol uptitration ongoing and Entresto + spironolactone on board, risk of hyperkalemia increases. Monitor BMP q3 months or sooner if medications change. If K >5.5, would consider reducing spironolactone first. Dietary potassium counseling — limit high-K foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, tomatoes). Patient to be given potassium-limited diet handout.
Bicarbonate 22 — low normal. If drops below 22 on repeat testing, will start sodium bicarbonate 650mg BID per KDIGO guidelines to slow CKD progression. Recheck with today's labs.

Empagliflozin 10mg would provide triple benefit: slows CKD progression (EMPA-KIDNEY), reduces HF hospitalization (EMPEROR-Reduced), and improves glycemic control. Will coordinate with cardiology (Dr. Wilson) and PCP (Dr. Chen) regarding initiation. Appropriate to start given eGFR >20. Discussed with patient — he is receptive but prefers to complete carvedilol uptitration first before adding another medication.
Return to nephrology in 6 months. Labs q3 months (CMP, CBC). UACR q6 months. Call if decreased urine output, new edema not responsive to diuretics, muscle cramps, or pruritus.

attestation:
I have personally evaluated this patient and agree with the documentation above.

Electronically signed by: Dr. Michael Torres
Date: 11/19/2021
